text: Hi, this is Jason Denbo, one of the HPB surgical oncologists at Moffitt Cancer Center. Today's case was an incidental pancreatic mass identified in the tail.

This lesion had a thickened wall that had arterial enhancement, and after further workup, the diagnosis was solidified as a non-functional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, approximately two point two centimeters in the tail of the pancreas.
We offered a robotic approach. This is the setup.
The robotic system is positioned from the right side, with four 8mm robotic trocars visible across the mid-abdomen and a 12mm assist port in place.
The surgeon prepares to extract the specimen through an extended supraumbilical port or a Pfannenstiel incision, utilizing the robotic setup with visible trocars and assist port.
The surgeon begins the procedure by placing a DEMA stitch to retract the left lobe of the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.

For years, I'd always used the Nathanson retractor or the snake retractor to retract the left lobe of the liver and the stomach. But I just found it to be cumbersome, and I commonly had to reposition the retractor throughout the procedure.
The DEMA stitch is visible, functioning as a hammock to support the left lateral segment of the liver, which appears small in size.
I've pretty much exclusively gone to using this retraction technique and no longer using external retractors.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer to mobilize the stomach, facilitating access to the surgical area.
The splenic flexure becomes visible as the surgeon approaches and divides the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon modifies the exposure to access and divide the remaining short gastric vessels attached to the superior pole of the spleen.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound to identify the tumor and surrounding vasculature, determining the planned transection plane. The stomach is retracted out of the way, and the infrapancreatic margin is mobilized.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to dissect within an avascular plane, continuing the lateral mobilization of the tissue.
The surgical field shows an avascular plane, with no visible blood vessels present in the area being dissected.

The distal transverse colon and splenic flexure are visible. The surgeon uses the hook cautery to dissect until encountering vessels within the splenocolic ligament, at which point the vessel sealer is employed.
The surgeon targets the mobilization of the inferior pole of the spleen, using the third robotic arm and assistant port to apply downward traction on the colon.

The surgeon continues the dissection, dividing the spinal colic attachments and the inferior pole of the spleen, followed by the spinal diaphragmatic attachments. The next step involves dissecting the splenic artery.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to locate the splenic artery, encircles it with a vessel loop for control, and prepares to divide it using a vascular stapler.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the suprapancreatic margin from the transection area of the splenic artery to the superior pole of the spleen. Additional mobilization off the retroperitoneum is performed to encircle the pancreas. The tumor is visible, bulging off the posterior aspect of the pancreas in the upper portion of the video.

The surgeon encircles the pancreas with umbilical tape to manipulate and orient it for division. The pancreas appears normal and thick, requiring two staple loads with a black stapler for complete division. Surgicel is applied to the pancreatic transection margin to control bleeding.

The surgeon completes the final retroperitoneal dissection to fully free the specimen, which is then placed into an endo-catch bag. The specimen is extracted through an extended supraumbilical port site, and a drain is subsequently placed.
He did very well and was discharged home on post-update three with a biochemical leak and his drain was removed in clinic.
Thank you for watching this video. Please like, comment, and share. And please subscribe to the channel so that you don't miss any of the upcoming new content.
